{"path": "workspace/python_guide.txt", "content": "================================================================================
                    PYTHON BEST PRACTICES & TESTING GUIDE
================================================================================

TABLE OF CONTENTS
-----------------
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
  1.1  Code Style & Formatting (PEP 8)
  1.2  Type Hints & Static Analysis
  1.3  Project Structure & Virtual Environments
  1.4  Documentation & Docstrings
  1.5  Error Handling & Logging
  1.6  Performance & Idiomatic Python
  1.7  Dependency Management
  1.8  Security Best Practices

PART 2: PYTHON TESTING FRAMEWORKS
  2.1  Overview of Major Frameworks
  2.2  unittest (Standard Library)
  2.3  pytest (Most Popular Third-Party)
  2.4  nose2 (Successor to nose)
  2.5  doctest (Tests in Documentation)
  2.6  Framework Comparison Table
  2.7  Testing Best Practices

================================================================================
PART 1: PYTHON BEST PRACTICES
================================================================================

1.1 CODE STYLE & FORMATTING (PEP 8)
------------------------------------
- Use 4 spaces per indentation level (never tabs).
- Limit lines to 79 characters for code, 72 for docstrings/comments.
- Use blank lines to separate top-level functions and class definitions:
    - 2 blank lines before top-level functions/classes.
    - 1 blank line before method definitions inside a class.
- Imports should be on separate lines and grouped in this order:
    1. Standard library imports
    2. Related third-party imports
    3. Local application/library-specific imports
- Avoid wildcard imports (from module import *).
- Naming conventions:
    - snake_case for functions, variables, and modules.
    - PascalCase (CapWords) for classes.
    - UPPER_CASE for constants.
    - _leading_underscore for internal/private use.
    - __double_underscore for name mangling (rarely needed).
- Use tools like black, autopep8, or ruff to auto-format code.
- Use flake8, pylint, or ruff for linting.

1.2 TYPE HINTS & STATIC ANALYSIS
----------------------------------
- Use type hints (PEP 484) to improve code clarity and catch bugs early:

    def greet(name: str) -> str:
        return f"Hello, {name}"

- Use Optional[X] instead of Union[X, None] (Python 3.10+ use X | None).
- Use collections.abc for generic collection types:
    - list[Item], dict[str, int], tuple[int, ...], set[str]
- Use mypy, pyright, or pyre for static type checking.
- Add type hints to function signatures and public APIs as a minimum.
- Use TypedDict for dictionaries with a fixed schema.
- Use Protocol for structural subtyping (duck typing with type safety).
- Use Literal for constrained string/enum-like values.

1.3 PROJECT STRUCTURE & VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENTS
----------------------------------------------
- Always use virtual environments (venv, virtualenv, or conda).
- Recommended project layout:

    my_project/
    ├── README.md
    ├── pyproject.toml          # Modern project metadata & config
    ├── src/
    │   └── my_package/
    │       ├── __init__.py
    │       ├── module.py
    │       └── subpackage/
    ├── tests/
    │   ├── __init__.py
    │   ├── test_module.py
    │   └── conftest.py         # pytest fixtures
    ├── docs/
    ├── scripts/
    └── .gitignore

- Use src-layout (src/ directory) to prevent accidental imports.
- Keep configuration in pyproject.toml (PEP 621).
- Use .gitignore to exclude __pycache__, .venv/, .env, *.pyc, etc.

1.4 DOCUMENTATION & DOCSTRINGS
--------------------------------
- Use docstrings for all public modules, functions, classes, and methods.
- Follow PEP 257 conventions. Common styles:
    - Google style (widely used, readable)
    - NumPy style (popular in scientific Python)
    - Sphinx/reStructuredText style
- Example (Google style):

    def calculate_total(items: list[
